sanskrit_devanagari:
अर्जुन उवाच ।
स्थितप्रज्ञस्य का भाषा समाधिस्थस्य केशव ।
स्थितधीः किं प्रभाषेत किमासीत व्रजेत किम् ॥ ५४ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
arjuna uvāca |
sthita-prajñasya kā bhāṣā samādhi-sthasya keśava |
sthita-dhīḥ kiṁ prabhāṣeta kim āsīta vrajeta kim || 54 ||

exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.54।। rather than ॥ ५४ ॥)
IAST exact (body text matches under standard romanization; raw_capture_path: data/sources/raw/bg-2-54-bgus.json)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.054 = MBh_06,024.054). Body text identical: 'sthitaprajñasya kā bhāṣā samādhisthasya keśava | sthitadhīḥ kiṁ prabhāṣeta kim āsīta vrajeta kim'. GRETIL writes the speaker tag 'arjuna uvāca' on the prior line, and writes 'sthitaprajñasya' / 'samādhisthasya' as single compounds (no hyphen); both are scribal-convention variants without semantic difference.

anvaya.meaning: of one of wisdom (in compound: 'of the steady-wisdomed one')
of one established (in compound: 'of one established in samādhi')

translations.verbatim_quote: Arjuna said: O Kṛṣṇa, what are the symptoms of one whose consciousness is thus merged in transcendence? How does he speak, and what is his language? How does he sit, and how does he walk?
Arjun said: O Keshav, what is the disposition of one who is situated in divine consciousness? How does an enlightened person talk? How does he sit? How does he walk?

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: As there are symptoms for each and every man, in terms of his particular situation, similarly one who is Kṛṣṇa conscious has his particular nature — talking, walking, thinking, feeling, etc. As a rich man has his symptoms by which he is known as a rich man, as a diseased man has his symptoms by which he is known as diseased…

O Keśava, kā, what; is the bhāṣā, description, the language (for the description) — how is he described by others —; sthita-prajñasya, of a man of steady wisdom, of one whose realization, 'I am the supreme Brahman', remains steady; samādhi-sthasya, of one who is Self-absorbed? Or kim, how; does the sthita-dhīḥ, dhīḥ, man of steady wisdom; himself prabhāṣeta, speak? How does he āsīta, sit? How does he vrajeta, move about? Through this verse Arjuna asks for a description of the man of steady wisdom.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as Arjuna's request for two distinct things: (a) the bhāṣā — the language / description by which others identify a steady-wisdomed sage; and (b) the sage's own conduct — how he himself speaks, sits, walks. The Advaita gloss makes sthita-prajña the one whose realization 'I am the supreme Brahman' (so'ham) remains unshaken. Arjuna is asking for behavioral marks that follow from that realization, so the sage can be recognised.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Arjuna said — What is the speech of a man of firm wisdom who is abiding with the mind controlled? What words can describe his state? What is his nature? This is the meaning of 'How does a man of firm wisdom speak etc.?' His specific conduct is now described as his nature can be inferred therefrom.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja frames Arjuna's question as a request for the inferred nature (svabhāva) of the firm-wisdomed one — what conduct allows us to infer the inner state. The Vishishtadvaita reading emphasises that the answer Krishna gives next will not just be a list of symptoms but a teaching: from outer conduct, one infers inner steadiness.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Desiring to know the characteristics of one who is self-realised in transcendental consciousness referred to in the previous verse, Arjuna asks: What is the definition of a person whose spiritual intelligence by nature resides in transcendence and is steady in wisdom? That by which something is described is definable and has characteristics.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Madhva's gloss makes explicit what the verse opens — Arjuna is asking for the definition (lakṣaṇa) of the sthita-prajña, a definitional inquiry into characteristics by which the sage is identifiable.

positions.rendering: disposition ('what is the disposition of one situated in divine consciousness')
description, the language by which he is described by others
speech / what words can describe his state

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: bhāṣā lexically means 'language / speech', but in the verse's question it functions ambiguously — Arjuna may be asking (a) how the sage himself speaks, (b) by what description others identify him, or (c) what defining marks (lakṣaṇa) characterise him. Shankara reads it as 'description by others' (matching the four follow-up actions: speech, sitting, walking — all observable from outside). Ramanuja reads it as the sage's own speech. Madhva collapses the question into a request for definition. Prabhupada's 'symptoms' is closest to Madhva's lakṣaṇa frame; Mukundananda's 'disposition' covers the inner-state interpretation. The verse text supports all three readings — Arjuna is asking a recognition question, and the answer Krishna gives in 2.55-2.72 covers both inner state and outer conduct.

positions.rendering: one whose consciousness is merged in transcendence (Krishna conscious)
one situated in divine consciousness / enlightened person
man of steady wisdom whose realization 'I am the supreme Brahman' remains steady
man of firm wisdom abiding with the mind controlled
person whose spiritual intelligence by nature resides in transcendence and is steady in wisdom

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The two terms sthita-prajña and sthita-dhīḥ are functionally synonymous in this verse — both name the steady-wisdomed/steady-minded sage. Where the traditions diverge is what 'steady' is anchored in: Shankara anchors it in Advaita non-dual realization; Ramanuja in mind-control with the self abiding in the Supreme; Madhva in spiritual intelligence by nature; the modern translators flatten to 'transcendence' / 'divine consciousness'. The verse itself does not constrain the metaphysical ground — it only asks for the recognisable marks. The opening of the sthita-prajña block is a definitional question, and each tradition will answer it according to its frame.

literal_meaning: Arjuna said: What is the language [or: description, defining mark] of the steady-wisdomed one, of one established in samādhi, O Keshava? How does the steady-minded one speak? How does he sit? How does he walk?
traditional_meaning_consensus: This verse opens the sthita-prajña block of Chapter 2 (verses 2.54-2.72). Krishna has just defined yoga as samatvam in 2.48 and the operator-state from which action issues; Arjuna now asks the natural follow-up — what does that operator actually look like? How is the steady-wisdomed sage recognised? Arjuna's question is double-edged: he asks both (a) by what description we identify such a person from outside, and (b) what their own conduct in speech, sitting, walking looks like. All three commentarial traditions read this as a definitional inquiry into lakṣaṇa (defining characteristics). The voice shifts: Krishna has been teaching imperatively (1.1-2.53); now the dialogue resumes — Arjuna interrupts to ask a recognition question, and Krishna will answer at length through the chapter's closing. The verse marks the chapter's third thread: the seasoned operator's state, described concretely.
